CLARITY.justification: The paper is overall organized into sensible sections (introduction, surface analysis, further analysis, related work, conclusion) and many core findings are stated clearly (e.g., sentence-length and POS differences in the Surface Analysis; model/feature results in Further Analysis). However the provided text contains many presentation problems: repeated and duplicated sentences and citation strings (e.g., multiple repeats of "Additionally, the ending is required..." and repeated SNLI/CNN/DailyMail/LAMBADA/SQuAD mentions), LaTeX artifacts and uneven references to figures/tables (Figure 1a/1b and Table 4 referenced but not shown here), which make parts hard to follow. Notation is minimal and mostly consistent, but the copy/paste and formatting errors degrade clarity and require cleanup.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of annotation/task-framing artifacts in NLP datasets and demonstrates it concretely on the ROC Story Cloze Task: a simple style-based linear classifier can distinguish original/right/wrong endings without context and yields a new state-of-the-art (the paper reports a substantial absolute improvement). By showing that different writing tasks impose measurable stylistic differences and can confound machine-reading evaluations (the authors explicitly note this methodological challenge), the results should change how researchers design and validate datasets. The work clearly opens follow-on directions (robust dataset construction, adversarial/evaluation protocols, cognitive studies of prompts) and has broader implications beyond this subfield.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper directly addresses a core NLP concern—dataset biases and evaluation artifacts—via a case study on the ROC Story Cloze task, showing that different writing prompts induce measurable stylistic confounds and that a style-based linear classifier achieves state-of-the-art on the task. The target audience (machine reading/comprehension and dataset-design researchers) is precisely the community for whom these findings matter, and the paper frames the problem and experiments (stylometric features, ablation of feature groups, comparisons to prior deep models) using methods and evaluation standards expected at top venues. The related-work and discussion sections explicitly position the work within both writing-style and machine-reading research, making it well suited for a top NLP/ML conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper offers a notable, novel perspective by demonstrating that subtle differences in writing tasks (original vs. right vs. wrong ROC story endings) induce measurable stylistic shifts and that a style-only linear model (character/word n-grams) can distinguish tasks and even attain state-of-the-art on the Story Cloze task. While the features and classifiers used are standard (logistic regression, char/word n-grams), framing dataset design as confounded by task-induced style and connecting this to deception/stylometry literature is a clear differentiation from prior work and would likely surprise practitioners—hence significant conceptual novelty despite limited methodological innovation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are solid and directly support the claims: surface analyses (length, POS and word distributions; Figures 1a/1b) and supervised classifiers (Experiment 1) distinguish original/right/wrong endings, and an ablation over feature groups (Table 4) shows character and word n-gram contributions. Ablation/sensitivity is present (feature subgroup experiments and inspection of top logistic coefficients), and related work is broad and fairly cited. Limitations are mentioned (e.g., caution about interpreting large logistic coefficients and dataset-design confounds) but failure cases and deeper error analysis are limited. Reproducibility is only partially supported—feature families and model types are described, but some implementation/hyperparameter details are incomplete (e.g., an unfinished learning-rate entry), which would hinder exact replication.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims—that different writing tasks induce measurable stylistic differences and that a simple style-based linear classifier can distinguish ending types and achieve state-of-the-art on the Story Cloze task—are clearly stated and supported by surface analyses (sentence length, POS and word distributions) and classification results (char/word n-gram performance, reported gains over prior work). There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check. However, the methodology lacks reported statistical significance tests and the provided text does not fully rule out confounds (e.g. worker/author overlap or dataset-generation artifacts) that could partially explain the style differences; the authors themselves note reliability issues with large coefficients on rare features. Overall the evidence supports the main empirical claims, but minor methodological gaps leave some conclusions (especially cognitive interpretations) less firmly established.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines for the Story Cloze task (e.g., Mostafazadeh et al. 2016a, Salle et al. 2016, LexVec/Speer 2016) and reports comparable metrics (accuracy / state-of-the-art numbers are cited). It analyzes differences in behavior vs prior methods (feature ablations, most-discriminative features, surface statistics) and situates results within relevant literature on style and deception. The manuscript does not fully spell out every experimental parity/detail (e.g., explicit confirmation of identical training/evaluation procedures for all baselines), so while comparisons appear fair and comprehensive, minor methodological clarity is missing.
